title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered in a charity bookshop. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Hannah, The Window Display Volunteer
Hannah started volunteering in the charity bookshop because she missed browsing second-hand novels after her favourite shop closed. She was not placed at the till, as she had originally expected, but asked to refresh the front window each week before opening on Mondays. [47]Her training as an art teacher helped her arrange books by colour, height and mood so that passers-by stopped rather than hurried past.[/47] At first she worried that the displays looked too playful for serious readers. [48]The turning point came when a customer entered only to buy the battered gardening book shown beside a cracked watering can.[/48] Hannah learned to treat donated objects as props that could tell a tiny story. She also discovered that faded covers often sold better when placed next to newer books with related themes. Now she photographs each window before changing it, partly to remember which combinations made people smile.
texts.B:
### Steve, The Sorting Room Volunteer
Steve worked in the sorting room behind the shop, opening bags of donations before they reached the shelves or the recycling boxes. [49]He was most surprised by the emotional difficulty of deciding what to recycle when a book clearly carried someone’s memories in its notes or inscriptions.[/49] For fairness, he began keeping a box of items that needed a second opinion. [50]His previous experience as a delivery driver helped him reorganise the cramped stockroom so volunteers could move boxes without blocking the fire exit.[/50] The simple improvement saved time and bruised knees. Steve enjoyed discovering odd objects tucked between pages, including bus tickets, recipes and one very old theatre programme. He says the hidden daily labour of a bookshop changed how he shops elsewhere; he now notices how much effort happens before anything looks calm at the front. He prefers the back room, where nobody complains about his radio.
texts.C:
### Priya, The Reading Group Organiser
Priya joined the bookshop when the manager asked if anyone could revive the small reading group that had stopped during building repairs the previous year. [51]She deliberately chose short, funny stories for the first meetings because she wanted nervous participants to finish the reading without feeling judged.[/51] The group grew very slowly, helped by tea, biscuits and a simple rule that nobody had to speak until they felt ready. [52]Priya’s biggest challenge was managing one confident member who answered every question before quieter readers had time to think.[/52] She solved it by asking people to write one sentence before discussion began. Priya was moved when a man who rarely made eye contact with anyone brought a poem he had copied out by hand. She now believes the group’s purpose is not literary expertise, but giving people a gentle weekly reason to leave the house on cold, wet evenings.
texts.D:
### Leo, The Online Sales Volunteer
Leo handled the shop’s online sales after noticing valuable specialist books were being priced like ordinary paperbacks. [53]He developed a habit of checking publication dates, signatures and missing dust jackets before anything unusual went onto the shelves.[/53] The careful work suited his patience, though accurately photographing books under the shop’s weak yellow lights was surprisingly frustrating. [54]He once accidentally listed a medical textbook under the wrong edition number and spent a weekend apologising to a disappointed buyer.[/54] After that embarrassing mistake he made a checklist and taped it beside the computer. Leo valued the messages from grateful collectors who explained why a particular title mattered to them. [55]He was especially pleased when an obscure railway manual sold for enough money to fund a month of the shop’s electricity.[/55] [56]He is training two volunteers to continue the listings, because online sales should not stop whenever he is away.[/56]

questions.question: used teaching experience to create displays that attracted people from outside?
questions.explanation: Hannah used her art teaching background to arrange books so passers-by stopped.

questions.question: remembers a sale that proved a display idea could work?
questions.explanation: Hannah saw the value of her display when a customer came in to buy the gardening book in the window.

questions.question: found it emotionally hard to discard items containing personal traces?
questions.explanation: Steve struggled to recycle books with notes or inscriptions showing someone’s memories.

questions.question: used a former driving job to improve movement through a storage area?
questions.explanation: Steve’s delivery experience helped him reorganise the stockroom so boxes did not block the fire exit.

questions.question: selected brief texts so new members would not feel under pressure?
questions.explanation: Priya chose short stories to help nervous participants finish the reading without feeling judged.

questions.question: had to deal with a participant who dominated group discussion?
questions.explanation: Priya found it challenging when one confident member answered every question first.

questions.question: checked specialist details before deciding how unusual books should be sold?
questions.explanation: Leo checked dates, signatures and dust jackets before unusual books went onto the shelves.

questions.question: made a public selling mistake involving the wrong version of a book?
questions.explanation: Leo listed a medical textbook under the wrong edition and had to apologise.

questions.question: mentions one sale that covered an important shop expense?
questions.explanation: Leo sold a railway manual for enough money to fund a month of electricity.

questions.question: is teaching others so the online work can continue during absences?
questions.explanation: Leo is training two volunteers so listings continue when he is away.
